questions.text: Sliding filament theory can be best explained as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Actin and myosin filaments do not shorten but rather slide pass each other
options.explanation: During muscle contraction, the laterally projecting heads (cross bridges) of the thick myosin myofilaments come in contact with the thin actin myofilaments and rotate on them. This pulls the thin myofilaments toward the middle of the sarcomere, past the thick myofilaments. The Z-lines come closer together and the sarcomere becomes shorter. Length of the A band remains constant. Myofilaments (both actin and myosin) stay the same length. Free ends of actin myofilaments move closer to the centre of the sarcomere, bringing Z-lines closer together. I bands shorten and H zone narrows. A similar action in all the sarcomeres results in shortening of the entire myofibril and thereby of the whole fibre and the whole muscle.

options.text: When myofilaments slide pass each other, myosin filaments shorten while actin filaments do not shorten
options.explanation: Both myosin and actin remain the same length, they just slide pass each other.

options.text: Troponin and myosin - Complex proteins in striated muscles
options.explanation: Skeletal muscle fibres occur in bundles and are normally attached to the skeleton. Each muscle fibre is an elongated cell surrounded externally by a delicate membrane, the sarcolemma. Just beneath the sarcolemma in each fibre many nuclei occur at irregular intervals. Thus, these fibres are mutlinucleated or syncytial in nature. The cytoplasm of each fibre (sarcoplasm) has a large number of myofibrils which are tightly packed. Each myofibril shows dark bands (A bands) containing myosin and light band (I bands) containing actin, alternating with each other. That is why these are named as striped muscle fibres. Actin filaments are thinner as compared to the myosin filaments. Each actin filament is made of two ‘F’ actins helically wound to each other. Each ‘F’ actin is a polymer of monomeric ‘G’ (globular) actins. Two filaments of another protein, tropomyosin also run close to the ‘F’ actins throughout its length. A complex protein troponin is distributed at regular intervals on the tropomyosin. In the resting state a subunit of troponin masks the active binding sites for myosin on the actin filaments. Each myosin filament is also a polymerised protein made up of many monomeric proteins called meromyosins.

There are four main types of diabetes: Type 1 Diabetes: This is an autoimmune condition in which the body's immune system attacks and destroys the insulin-producing beta cells in the pancreas. As a result, people with type 1 diabetes produce little to no insulin, and they require lifelong insulin therapy to regulate their blood sugar levels. Type 2 Diabetes: This is the most common type of diabetes and occurs when the body becomes resistant to the effects of insulin or doesn't produce enough insulin to meet the body's needs. Type 2 diabetes is often associated with lifestyle factors such as obesity, sedentary behavior, and poor diet. It can be managed with lifestyle changes, oral medications, and, in some cases, insulin therapy. Gestational Diabetes: This type of diabetes occurs during pregnancy when the body cannot produce enough insulin to meet the increased insulin needs of the mother and the developing baby. Gestational diabetes usually resolves after childbirth, but women who have had gestational diabetes are at increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes later in life. Diabetes Insipidus: This is a less common form of diabetes unrelated to blood sugar regulation. Diabetes insipidus is caused by a deficiency of antidiuretic hormone (ADH), which leads to excessive thirst and frequent urination. It can be caused by damage to the pituitary gland or the kidneys and is managed by replacing the missing ADH hormone.

The cervical vertebrae are numbered C1 to C7, and they are responsible for supporting the head and allowing for movement and flexibility. No, the number is not correct. Humans typically have 12 pairs of ribs, and only the last two pairs are referred to as "floating ribs." The human ribcage is composed of 12 pairs of ribs, with each rib attaching to the thoracic vertebrae at the back and curving around to the front, where most ribs connect to the sternum through cartilage. The first seven pairs of ribs are known as "true ribs" because they directly connect to the sternum. The next three pairs of ribs (8th, 9th, and 10th) are called "false ribs" because their cartilages attach indirectly to the sternum. The last two pairs of ribs (11th and 12th) are referred to as "floating ribs" because they do not connect to the sternum at all and only attach to the vertebrae at the back. So, the correct number of "floating ribs" in humans is two pairs, not four pairs.

questions.text: The contractile protein of skeletal muscle involving ATPase activity is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Troponin is a regulatory protein in muscle tissue, particularly in skeletal and cardiac muscles. It plays a role in controlling muscle contractions by regulating the interaction between actin and myosin. While it is essential for muscle contraction, it does not have ATPase activity.

Tropomyosin is another regulatory protein that is involved in muscle contraction. It works in conjunction with troponin to regulate the interaction between actin and myosin. Like troponin, it does not possess ATPase activity.

Myosin is a contractile protein found in both skeletal and smooth muscle. It is the primary motor protein responsible for muscle contraction. Myosin has ATPase activity, which is crucial for the energy-dependent process of muscle contraction. It hydrolyzes ATP to provide the energy required for muscle fibers to contract.

Alpha-actinin is not a contractile protein but rather a structural protein found in muscle fibers. It plays a role in anchoring actin filaments to the Z-discs in muscle sarcomeres. It does not have ATPase activity and is not directly involved in the sliding filament theory of muscle contraction.

questions.explanation: Myosin is a contractile protein that interacts with actin to bring about contraction of muscle or cell movement. Troponin, tropomyosin and actinin are the actin in the thin filament.

questions.explanation: In contraction, the laterally projecting heads (cross-bridges) of the thick myosin myofilaments come in contact with the thin actin myofilaments and rotate on them. questions.text: Cartilage is covered by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The periosteum is a layer of dense connective tissue that covers the outer surface of bones, except at the joints. It consists of two layers: an outer fibrous layer and an inner osteogenic layer. The fibrous layer contains collagen fibers that help to anchor the periosteum to the bone, while the osteogenic layer contains cells that can differentiate into osteoblasts, which are cells that produce new bone tissue. The periosteum also contains blood vessels and nerves that supply the bone with oxygen and nutrients and help to regulate bone growth and repair.

It is a layer of dense connective tissue that surrounds most cartilage in the body. The perichondrium contains blood vessels and stem cells that can differentiate into chondroblasts, which are cells that produce new cartilage tissue. The perichondrium also helps to protect and nourish the cartilage and provides a site for the attachment of ligaments and tendons.

The perimysium is a layer of connective tissue that surrounds a bundle of muscle fibers (known as a fascicle) in skeletal muscle. It is composed of collagen fibers and elastic fibers, which provide structural support to the muscle fibers and help to distribute the force generated by muscle contraction. The perimysium also contains blood vessels and nerves that supply the muscle fibers with oxygen and nutrients and help to control muscle contraction.

The perineurium is a layer of connective tissue that surrounds a bundle of nerve fibers (known as a fascicle) in a peripheral nerve. It is composed of several layers of flattened cells, which are held together by tight junctions that form a barrier called the blood-nerve barrier. The perineurium helps to protect the nerve fibers from damage and regulates the exchange of nutrients and other substances between the blood vessels and the nerve tissue.

questions.explanation: Perichondrium is covering around cartilage. It is a layer of dense connective tissue that surrounds most cartilage in the body. The perichondrium contains blood vessels and stem cells that can differentiate into chondroblasts, which are cells that produce new cartilage tissue. The perichondrium also helps to protect and nourish the cartilage and provides a site for the attachment of ligaments and tendons.

questions.text: Major protein of connective tissues is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The major protein of connective tissue is collagen. Collagen is a fibrous protein that provides strength and support to tissues such as skin, bone, cartilage, tendons, and ligaments. It is the most abundant protein in the human body and is composed of three polypeptide chains that are tightly coiled together to form a triple helix. There are many different types of collagen, each with a unique structure and function, but all share the characteristic triple helix structure.

Actin is a protein that is found in all eukaryotic cells and is a major component of the cytoskeleton. The cytoskeleton is a network of protein filaments that provides structural support to the cell and helps to maintain its shape. Actin filaments are thin, flexible fibers that can contract and expand, allowing the cell to move and change shape. In addition to its role in the cytoskeleton, actin is also involved in many other cellular processes, including cell division, cell signaling, and muscle contraction.

Titin, also known as connectin, is a giant protein that is found in muscle tissue. It is the largest known protein, with a molecular weight of approximately 3.7 million Daltons. Titin spans the entire length of a sarcomere, which is the basic unit of muscle contraction, and helps to maintain the structural integrity of the sarcomere. It is composed of multiple repeating units called immunoglobulin (Ig) domains, which are connected by flexible regions that allow the protein to stretch and recoil. Titin also interacts with other muscle proteins, such as myosin and actin, to regulate muscle contraction.

Albumin is a protein that is produced in the liver and is found in blood plasma. It is the most abundant protein in the blood and plays an important role in maintaining the osmotic pressure of the blood and transporting various substances, such as hormones, fatty acids, and drugs, throughout the body. Albumin also acts as a buffer, helping to maintain the pH of the blood within a narrow range. In addition, albumin is involved in immune function and can bind and transport certain toxins and waste products for excretion by the kidneys.

questions.explanation: Collagen is the most abundant protein in animal's body. Actin is most abundant in muscles and albumin is most abundant in blood plasma. Titin is present in skeletal muscle cell and it is the largest protein.

options.explanation: A ball and socket joint is a type of joint where the rounded end of one bone fits into a cup-like depression in another bone. questions.text: Antibodies attack joints in:

options.explanation: Osteoarthritis is a type of arthritis that is caused by wear and tear on the joints over time. It is the most common type of arthritis, and it typically affects older adults. In osteoarthritis, the cartilage that cushions the joints begins to break down, leading to pain, stiffness, and reduced mobility. Over time, the joint may become damaged and deformed.

Juvenile arthritis is a type of arthritis that occurs at young age. There are several different types of juvenile arthritis, but all involve inflammation of the joints and can cause pain, swelling, and stiffness.

Gouty arthritis is a type of arthritis that is caused by a buildup of uric acid crystals in the joints. Uric acid is a waste product that is normally excreted by the kidneys, but in some people, it can build up in the blood and form crystals in the joints.

In rheumatoid arthritis, the immune system mistakenly attacks the lining of the joints, causing inflammation and damage to the joint tissue. Antibodies called rheumatoid factors and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are often present in the blood of people with rheumatoid arthritis, but these antibodies do not directly attack the joints. Instead, they are markers of the autoimmune response that is causing the inflammation and joint damage.

It anchors the thin filaments and serves as the attachment point for the structural proteins that hold the sarcomere together. During muscle contraction, the Z lines are pulled closer together as the sarcomeres shorten.

The M line is a thin line found in the center of the A band. It is made up of structural proteins that help hold the thick filaments together and maintain their alignment during muscle contraction. In the context of muscle anatomy, the M line (also known as the M band) is a structure found in the center of the A band in the middle of the sarcomere.

The A band is a dark-staining band in the sarcomere and represents the region where thick and thin filaments overlap. It is composed mainly of thick filaments, which are made up of myosin protein. During muscle contraction, the A band remains constant in length, as the thick filaments do not change in length during the sliding of thin filaments.

options.explanation: The Z line is a dense, dark-staining line that bisects the I band and serves as the boundary between adjacent sarcomeres. It anchors the thin filaments and serves as the attachment point for the structural proteins that hold the sarcomere together. During muscle contraction, the Z lines are pulled closer together as the sarcomeres shorten.
The M line is a thin line found in the center of the A band. It is made up of structural proteins that help hold the thick filaments together and maintain their alignment during muscle contraction.

T-tubules (transverse tubules) in human skeletal muscles are present at the A-I junction, which refers to the area where the A band (anisotropic band) and the I band (isotropic band) meet within the sarcomere. T-tubules are invaginations of the sarcolemma (cell membrane) that penetrate deep into the muscle fiber, allowing for rapid transmission of action potentials (electrical signals) from the surface of the muscle cell to the interior. They play a critical role in coordinating muscle contraction by ensuring that the electrical signal reaches all parts of the muscle fiber simultaneously. At the A-I junction, the T-tubules are strategically located near the Z line (Z disc), which is the boundary between adjacent sarcomeres. This positioning allows the T-tubules to be in close proximity to both the A band and the I band, where the contractile elements of the sarcomere (thick and thin filaments) are located.

The H zone is a region within the A band where only thick filaments (myosin) are present without overlapping thin filaments. It is located in the center of the A band and narrows during muscle contraction as the thin filaments slide inward and overlap with the thick filaments.

These globular monomers have specific binding sites for myosin and can polymerize to form long chains called F-actin (filamentous actin), which is a major component of the thin filaments involved in muscle contraction.

questions.text: A muscle protein that has 3 polypeptides:

options.explanation: Actin consists of 1 polypeptide chain.
Myosin consists of 6 polypeptide chains.

questions.text: Cross bridges form between:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cross bridges do not form between troponin and tropomyosin. Troponin and tropomyosin are regulatory proteins that play roles in controlling the interaction between actin and myosin during muscle contraction. Cross bridges involve interactions between the myosin heads and actin filaments.

This is the correct answer. Cross bridges form between actin filaments and myosin heads. During muscle contraction, myosin heads attach to binding sites on actin filaments, forming cross bridges. This attachment and subsequent movement of the myosin heads create the sliding filament mechanism, leading to muscle contraction.

Cross bridges do not form between calcium and sodium. Calcium ions play a crucial role in initiating muscle contraction by binding to troponin, which leads to the exposure of myosin-binding sites on actin. Sodium ions are involved in generating action potentials that stimulate muscle contraction, but they do not directly form cross bridges.

Cross bridges do not form between the sarcolemma (cell membrane of muscle fibers) and the sarcoplasmic reticulum (an organelle involved in calcium storage and release). These structures are involved in the regulation of muscle contraction, particularly in terms of calcium ion concentration, but they do not directly form cross bridges.

questions.explanation: Cross bridges form between actin filaments and myosin heads. During muscle contraction, myosin heads attach to binding sites on actin filaments, forming cross bridges. This attachment and subsequent movement of the myosin heads create the sliding filament mechanism, leading to muscle contraction.

questions.text: When a muscle is at rest, what blocks myosin from heading to actin?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. When a muscle is at rest, tropomyosin is a regulatory protein that covers the myosin-binding sites on the actin filament. This covering prevents the myosin heads from attaching to actin, effectively inhibiting muscle contraction. Tropomyosin's position is controlled by the troponin complex in response to calcium levels.

"Tubulin" is a protein that makes up microtubules, which are part of the cytoskeleton and have various roles in cellular processes. However, tubulin is not directly related to muscle contraction or the blocking of myosin binding to actin.

Troponin is a regulatory protein complex that plays a crucial role in muscle contraction, particularly in response to calcium ions. Troponin binds to actin and tropomyosin, and its conformational changes are responsible for exposing the myosin-binding sites on actin. It does not directly block myosin from binding to actin.

A sarcomere is the basic contractile unit of a muscle fiber and includes actin and myosin filaments. While the sarcomere is where muscle contraction occurs, it is not what blocks myosin from binding to actin when the muscle is at rest.

questions.text: All of the following are true regarding muscle contraction except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is true. During muscle contraction, the I band (isotropic band) shortens. The I band contains only thin (actin) filaments and is located between sarcomeres. As the sarcomeres contract, the distance between the Z-lines decreases, causing the I band to shorten.
This statement is accurate. The A band (anisotropic band), which contains overlapping thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments, remains relatively constant in length during muscle contraction.

This statement is true. Z-lines (also known as Z-discs) mark the boundaries of individual sarcomeres. During muscle contraction, the Z-lines of adjacent sarcomeres get closer together as the sarcomeres shorten.

This statement is not accurate. The M-line is a structure in the center of the sarcomere where myosin filaments are anchored. It does not disappear during muscle contraction; it remains in place, providing a central reference point for the organization of myosin filaments.

questions.text: Contractile protein of skeletal muscle cells involving ATPase activity is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Actin is another contractile protein in muscle cells, but it does not possess ATPase activity. Actin's primary role is to interact with myosin during the sliding filament mechanism of muscle contraction.

This is the correct answer. Myosin is a contractile protein found in muscle cells, and it contains ATPase activity. The myosin molecule has a globular head region with ATPase activity, which plays a critical role in the energy-dependent cycle of muscle contraction. Myosin ATPase hydrolyzes ATP to provide the energy required for the myosin heads to attach to actin, generate force, and initiate muscle contraction.

Troponin is not a contractile protein involved in ATPase activity. Instead, troponin is a regulatory protein complex that regulates the interaction between actin, myosin, and calcium ions during muscle contraction.
Tropomyosin is also not a contractile protein with ATPase activity. Tropomyosin is a regulatory protein that helps control the interaction between actin and myosin during muscle contraction.

questions.text: Which triggers the release of calcium ions from: sarcoplasmic reticulum?

This is the correct answer. An action potential, which is an electrical signal generated by nerve cells, triggers the release of calcium ions from the sarcoplasmic reticulum. The action potential travels along the sarcolemma (muscle cell membrane) and down the T-tubules, leading to the activation of calcium-release channels in the sarcoplasmic reticulum called ryanodine receptors (RYR). These channels open, allowing calcium ions to flow from the sarcoplasmic reticulum into the cytoplasm of the muscle cell.

questions.text: According to sliding filament theory, when muscle fibers are stimulated by nervous system, which of the following changes occurs?

options.explanation: This statement is true. According to the sliding filament theory, during muscle contraction, the I-bands (isotropic bands), which contain only thin (actin) filaments, shorten. As the sarcomeres contract, the thin filaments slide inward, causing the I-bands to become smaller.

This statement is false. During muscle contraction, the Z-lines (Z-discs), which mark the boundaries of individual sarcomeres, move closer together as the sarcomeres shorten. The Z-lines do not move further apart.

This statement is false. The H-zone is the region in the center of the sarcomere where only thick (myosin) filaments are present. During muscle contraction, the H-zone becomes less visible or may even disappear as the thin filaments overlap with the thick filaments.

questions.text: It acts as an immediate source of energy for muscle contraction.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: ATP is the immediate source of energy for muscle contraction. Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is a molecule found in cells that serves as a universal energy carrier. Its structure includes three phosphate groups. When a phosphate group is cleaved from ATP, energy is released. This energy is used to power various cellular processes, including muscle contraction. During muscle contraction, ATP is hydrolyzed to ADP (adenosine diphosphate) and inorganic phosphate (Pi). The energy released during this hydrolysis is utilized to fuel the interaction between actin and myosin, allowing muscle fibers to contract.

Phosphocreatine, or creatine phosphate, is a molecule stored in muscle cells that acts as a quick energy reserve. During intense muscle activity, ATP is rapidly used up. Phosphocreatine steps in to donate a phosphate group to ADP, converting it back to ATP. This helps sustain short bursts of high-energy contractions, like those seen in activities such as sprinting or weightlifting. While phosphocreatine aids in ATP regeneration, it is not the primary source of energy for muscle contraction; ATP itself directly provides the energy through its phosphate bonds' hydrolysis.

Fatty acids are not an immediate source of energy for muscle contraction. While fatty acids can provide a substantial amount of energy during prolonged, low-intensity exercise, their utilization requires more complex metabolic pathways and is not suitable for rapid, short bursts of muscle contraction.

Glycogen is a stored form of glucose in muscles and liver. It can be broken down into glucose, which can then enter glycolysis and other metabolic pathways to produce ATP. While glycogen is an important energy source during longer durations of exercise, it is not the immediate source of energy for rapid muscle contractions.

questions.text: When muscle contracts, thick and thin filaments undergo:

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. During muscle contraction, the thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments undergo overlapping. The sliding filament mechanism describes how the myosin heads of the thick filaments attach to the actin filaments and pull them toward the center of the sarcomere, causing the filaments to overlap. This overlapping is a fundamental part of the mechanism that generates muscle force and contraction.

Shortening of the filaments does occur as the muscle contracts, but it's not the primary change that occurs. The shortening of sarcomeres (the repeating units of muscle fibers) as a result of the overlapping of filaments leads to the shortening of the entire muscle fiber, which results in muscle contraction. However, shortening itself doesn't fully encompass the changes that occur during contraction.

Lengthening of the filaments is not a typical occurrence during muscle contraction. Muscle contraction involves the actin and myosin filaments sliding past each other, leading to overlapping and shortening of the sarcomeres and muscle fibers.

While "contraction" is the general term used to describe the process of muscle shortening and generating force, it doesn't capture the specific changes that occur at the filament level during contraction. The process of muscle contraction involves various structural changes within the sarcomeres, including overlapping of thick and thin filaments.

questions.text: The function of calcium ions in muscle contraction is to:

When calcium ions bind to troponin, it induces a conformational change in the troponin-tropomyosin complex. This change exposes the myosin-binding sites on the actin filaments, allowing the myosin heads to form cross-bridges and initiate muscle contraction.

This is the correct answer. Skeletal muscles are the muscles that are attached to the skeleton through tendons. They are responsible for voluntary movements, such as walking, jumping, and lifting. Skeletal muscles are under conscious control and play a crucial role in body movement and maintaining posture.

Cardiac muscles are found in the heart and are responsible for pumping blood. They are involuntary muscles that work continuously to maintain the heart's rhythm and function. While cardiac muscles are not attached to the skeleton, they play a vital role in cardiovascular function.

A tendon is a bundle of collagen fibers that attach muscle to bone. It is a non-elastic tissue, which means that it does not stretch or recoil. This makes it strong and durable, allowing it to transmit the force of muscle contraction to the bone. Tendons are found throughout the body, connecting muscles to bones in the limbs, torso, and head. They are essential for movement, allowing us to walk, run, jump, and perform other activities. Tendons can be injured by overuse, trauma, or disease. Overuse injuries, such as tendinitis and tendinosis, can occur when tendons are subjected to repetitive stress.

questions.text: It is a long cylindrical cell with multiple oval nuclei arranged just beneath its sarcolemma:

options.explanation: A muscle fiber is a long, cylindrical cell that is the basic unit of skeletal muscle tissue. It is made up of many smaller cells that have fused together. Each muscle fiber has multiple nuclei, which are located just beneath the cell membrane, called the sarcolemma. Muscle fibers are very long and can be up to several centimeters in length. They are also very thin, with a diameter of only about 10 micrometers. This allows them to be packed together tightly in muscles, which gives muscles their strength and power. Muscle fibers are also very specialized for contraction. They contain proteins called actin and myosin, which form the contractile units of the muscle cell. When actin and myosin filaments slide past each other, they cause the muscle fiber to contract.

The perimysium helps to protect the muscle fibers and to transmit force from the muscle fibers to the tendons.

options.explanation: Muscle tissue contains something called muscle fibers. Muscle fibers consist of a single muscle cell. They help to control the physical forces within the body. When grouped together, they can facilitate organized movement of your limbs and tissues.

A sarcomere is the basic contractile unit of a myocyte (muscle fiber). A sarcomere is composed of two main protein filaments (thin actin and thick myosin filaments) which are the active structures responsible for muscular contraction.

Each compartment contains a bundle of muscle fibers. Each bundle of muscle fiber is called a fasciculus and is surrounded by a layer of connective tissue called the perimysium. Within the fasciculus, each individual muscle cell, called a muscle fiber, is surrounded by connective tissue called the endomysium.

Myofibrils are long contractile fibers, groups of which run parallel to each other on the long axis of the myocytes (long single multinucleated cells that combine to form the muscle). The myocytes run parallel to each other on the long axis of the cell.

questions.explanation: Each muscle consists of muscle bundles, which are further composed of muscle fibers of cells.

A Golgi body, also known as a Golgi apparatus, is a cell organelle that helps process and package proteins and lipid molecules, especially proteins destined to be exported from the cell. Named after its discoverer, Camillo Golgi, the Golgi body appears as a series of stacked membranes.

Microfilaments, also called actin filaments, consist of two intertwined strands of a globular protein known as actin. They are the polymers of the protein actin and are the smallest filaments of the cytoskeleton. They have a vital role in cell movements, cell division, and muscle contraction.

The sarcoplasmic reticulum is a complex network of specialized smooth endoplasmic reticulum that is important in transmitting electrical impulses as well as in the storage of calcium ions. These longitudinal tubules form a membrane-bound system of tubules and cisterns that surround the myocytes.

questions.text: Haversian canals occur in the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Small bones are solid. Long bones, such as the humerus and femur, have a cavity, the marrow cavity, at the centre. The substance of the bone is distinguishable into 3 regions: the periosteum, matrix and endosteum. The matrix of bone along with the bone-forming cells (osteoblasts) is arranged in concentric layers (lamellae) round the small canals which run parallel to the long axis (shaft) of the bone. These canals, called Haversian canals, are interconnected with one another via Volkmann's canals and contain a blood vessel, a nerve and a lymph vessel. Bone cells remain alive and once they are surrounded by the hard bone matrix, they are called osteocytes. The osteocytes are embedded in fluid-filled cavities within the concentric lamellae. These cavities are known as lacunae and occur at regular intervals in these concentric layers of bone tissue. The lacunae are connected to one another and to the Haversian canals by a system of interconnecting canals known as canaliculi. Each Haversian canal, its concentric lamellae, lacunae with osteocytes and canaliculi forms a long cylinder and is called a Haversian system. Separate Haversian systems are joined to each other by means of interstitial lamellae.
This is incorrect.

questions.text: Stratum germinativum is an example of which kind of epithelium?

options.explanation: Stratum germinativum (also stratum basale or basal cell layer) is the layer of keratinocytes that lies at the base of the epidermis immediately above the dermis. It consists of a single layer of tall, simple columnar epithelial cells lying on a basement membrane. These cells undergo rapid cell division, mitosis to replenish the regular loss of skin by shedding from the surface. About 25% of the cells are melanocytes, which produce melanin which provides pigmentation for skin and hair.

options.explanation: Epithelial cells are specialized cells that form the outer layer of surfaces and linings in the body, serving as a protective barrier. They are found in skin, organs, and various tissues, playing crucial roles in absorption, secretion, and sensation.

Muscle cells, also known as muscle fibers, are the contractile cells responsible for movement in the body. questions.text: In which one of the following preparations are you likely to come across cell junctions most frequently?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: From the given four options, cell junctions come across most frequently in the preparation of ciliated epithelium. A cell junction is a structure within a tissue of a multicellular organism. Cell junctions are especially abundant in epithelial tissues. They consist of protein complexes and provide contact between neighbouring cells, between a cell and the extracellular matrix, or they build up the paracellular barrier of epithelia and control the paracellular transport. Ciliated epithelium is a region of epithelium consisting of columnar or cuboidal cells bearing hairlike appendages that are capable of beating rapidly. Ciliated epithelium performs the function of moving particles or fluid over the epithelial surface in such structures as the trachea, bronchial tubes, and nasal cavities. It often occurs in the vicinity of mucus-secreting goblet cells. The other three (thrombocytes, tendon and hyaline cartilage) belong to connective tissue. Connective tissue is widely distributed and has many functions including support, packing, defence and repair. Thrombocytes or platelets aid the formation of blood clots by releasing various protein substances. Tendon attaches a muscle to a bone and hyaline cartilage consists largely of glycosaminoglycan, giving a shiny glass-like appearance and gives flexibility and support at the joints.

It is of four types- hyaline, fibrous, calcified and elastic. Yellow, elastic fibrocartilage is found in the pinna and external auditory canals of the ear, Eustachian tubes, epiglottis and the tip of the nose. Its matrix contains numerous yellow fibres which form a network by uniting with one another. Due to the presence of yellow fibres, the cartilage becomes more flexible. Hence, it provides flexibility to these organs.

options.explanation: Bulliform cells, also known as motor cells, are huge, bubble-shaped epidermal cells found on the top surface of leaves in groups of multiple monocots. These cells are found on the top or adaxial leaf surface. They are usually found at the midvein part of the leaf.

Lenticels are visible on fruit surfaces, such as mango, apple, and avocado. Lenticels permit the exchange of gases between the environment and the internal tissue spaces of the organs (stems and some fruits) (Fig. 6.2). They permit the entrance of oxygen and simultaneously the output of carbon dioxide and water vapor.
Complementary cells are a mass of cells in plants, formed from the cork cambium at the position of the lenticels. It is a group of loosely arranged cells that aid in gaseous exchange through cork.

Subsidiary cells. A stoma (singular for stomata) is surrounded by two types of specialized plant cells that differ from other plant epidermal cells. These cells are called guard cells and subsidiary cells. Subsidiary cells, also called accessory cells, surround and support guard cells.

questions.text: Which of the following plant tissues is composed of dead cells?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The epidermis is the outermost layer of cells in plant organs such as stems, leaves, and roots. It acts as a protective barrier against water loss, pathogens, and mechanical damage. The epidermis is typically composed of living cells, including specialized cells like guard cells, trichomes, and root hairs, which are involved in various functions such as gas exchange and absorption.

Parenchyma is a type of ground tissue found throughout the plant. It is composed of living cells that have thin cell walls and large intercellular spaces. Parenchyma cells have diverse functions depending on their location in the plant, such as storage of nutrients, photosynthesis, and tissue repair.

Meristem refers to the plant tissue responsible for the growth and development of new cells. Meristematic cells are undifferentiated and actively divide, giving rise to various specialized cells and tissues. Since meristems are involved in cell division and growth, the cells in this tissue are generally alive.

Sclerenchyma is a type of ground tissue characterized by thick, lignified cell walls. These walls provide rigidity and support to plant organs. Sclerenchyma cells are typically dead at maturity and serve structural roles, such as providing strength to the plant. Examples of sclerenchyma cells include fibers and sclereids.

questions.explanation: Sclerenchyma is a plant tissue that consists of dead cells at maturity. These cells have thick, lignified cell walls, providing strength and support to the plant. Examples of sclerenchyma cells include fibers and sclereids.
